topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I watched Sarah meticulously recount the triumphs of her new marketing position, her gestures wide and practiced. Every time she mentioned the 'innovative strategy' or the 'challenging client portfolio,' a dull ache settled behind my ribs. I just nodded, the movement slow, deliberate. I’d seen the subtle tremor in her hand when she talked about her commute, the way her eyes flickered toward the door as if expecting an interruption. It was exhausting, this constant performance of authenticity. I felt the familiar weight of knowing the truth, a heavy, unmovable stone in my chest. I took a slow sip, letting the silence stretch, unwilling to break the fragile peace she was constructing around this fabrication.

[story 2]

I stared at the spreadsheet on my screen, the numbers blurring into meaningless patterns. Liam was telling me about his promotion, his ascent in the tech sector, and I could see the sheen of forced excitement on his face. He spoke of late nights and groundbreaking achievements, but the details felt hollow, like echoes in an empty room. I didn't challenge him. Instead, I meticulously reorganized my own files, the clicking of the keyboard a steady, monotonous rhythm. There was no surge of anger, only a deep, settling quietude. I just accepted the landscape as it was: Liam was living in a world I couldn't access, and I was simply here, watching the façade hold.

[story 3]

The air in the cafe felt thick, suffocating. Maya was laughing, a bright, tinkling sound that grated on my nerves. She was describing her demanding new role with such vivid enthusiasm, painting a picture of success I couldn't quite reconcile with the quiet, slightly frayed edges of the reality I’d observed. I kept my gaze fixed on the condensation tracking down my glass. When she paused for breath, I let my gaze drift to the slight catch in her voice, the way her composure momentarily slipped. I didn't say anything. I just picked up my bag, the movement economical and devoid of energy. It was a recognition, cold and absolute, that some truths are too heavy to carry, and some lies are just easier to bear.
